flashcards.front: What is a frame in paging?
flashcards.back: A frame is a fixed-size block of physical memory. It has the same size as a page and is the unit of memory allocation in a paging system.

flashcards.front: What is frame allocation in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Frame allocation is the process of deciding how to distribute available physical memory frames among competing processes in a paging system.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of frames required for a process?
flashcards.back: The minimum number of frames is determined by the maximum number of pages a process can reference in any single instruction (i.e., the number of pages needed to store the instruction and all its operands).
Fixed allocation gives each process a predetermined number of frames at system design time, regardless of process size or priority.
Proportional allocation assigns frames to processes based on their size - larger processes get more frames proportionally to their memory requirements.
Priority allocation gives more frames to higher-priority processes, allowing them to keep more pages in memory and execute faster.
Global allocation allows processes to compete for all available frames in the system; a process can take frames from other processes when page faults occur.
Local allocation restricts each process to using only its own allocated frames; a process cannot steal frames from other processes.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between number of frames and page faults?
flashcards.back: As the number of frames allocated to a process increases, the page fault rate initially decreases sharply, then levels off. Too few frames causes thrashing.
Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing instructions due to insufficient frames.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: The working set model defines the set of pages a process is currently using. The OS tries to keep these pages in memory to minimize page faults.

flashcards.front: What is page fault frequency (PFF) control?
flashcards.back: PFF is a frame allocation strategy that monitors the page fault rate of processes and adjusts their frame allocation to keep the fault rate within acceptable bounds.

flashcards.front: Why is balanced frame allocation important?
flashcards.back: Balanced allocation prevents thrashing, reduces overall page faults, improves system throughput, and ensures fair distribution of memory resources among processes.

flashcards.front: What happens when all frames are allocated and a page fault occurs?
flashcards.back: When all frames are occupied, the system must perform page replacement - evicting a page from some process to make room for the new page.

flashcards.front: How does proportional allocation calculate frames for a process?
flashcards.back: Frames for process i = (size of process i / total size of all processes) × total free frames in system.
